fact: August 2 is exactly 214 days into the year, which means there are only 151 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is typically peak butterfly season - monarchs are busy stuffing their faces with nectar to fuel up for their epic migration south.
The sun usually rises around 6 AM and sets around 8 PM in most of North America, giving you about 14 hours of daylight to play outside.
August 2 falls in the middle of Leo season in the zodiac - people born on this day are supposedly as dramatic and attention-loving as a roaring lion.
Cicadas are often screaming their loudest right now in many places, creating a buzzing symphony that can reach 120 decibels - louder than a chainsaw!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, August 2 is deep winter, with temperatures that can drop below freezing while kids in America are sweating at the beach.
This date sits exactly 44 days after the summer solstice, meaning daylight is already shrinking by about 2 minutes per day in northern regions.
Fireflies are typically putting on their best light shows around this time, blinking in perfect patterns to find mates in the warm summer darkness.
August 2 often marks the height of thunderstorm season in many parts of the world, when hot air collides with cool fronts to create spectacular lightning displays.
Corn plants in the Midwest are usually growing so fast they make crackling sounds you can actually hear - some farmers say they grow 3 inches in a single day around this time.
The Perseid meteor shower is just getting started, with space rocks beginning to streak across the sky as Earth plows through a trail of comet debris.
In northern Canada and Alaska, the sun barely sets at all on August 2, creating an eerie twilight that lasts almost all night long.
